meta_title: For Students: analytics setup to Solve no portfolio (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to analytics setup and overcome no portfolio. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: Analytics Setup to Solve No Portfolio
content.seo_description: Learn how to perform an analytics setup to overcome the no portfolio problem. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code for your career.
content.answer_block: To solve the no-portfolio gap, integrate robust analytics into your student project. By capturing real-time user behavior, you transform a static 'code exercise' into a data-driven product. This evidence-based approach proves to employers that you understand how to measure success, optimize user experience, and drive business outcomes effectively.
content.intro:
You stare at a blank GitHub profile, the silence of 'no activity' echoing louder than your actual coding skills. You have built projects, but they sit in a digital vacuum, invisible to recruiters and lacking the validation of real-world usage. You aren't just missing a portfolio; you are missing the story of your impact.

The secret isn't building more apps; it is instrumenting the ones you have. By implementing professional-grade analytics, you shift the narrative from 'I wrote this code' to 'I optimized this user journey.' This transition turns your beginner projects into case studies that scream professional competence.

steps.title: Define Your Key Metrics
steps.description: Before writing code, identify the 'North Star' metric for your project. Are you tracking button clicks, signup conversion, or time-on-page?
steps.antigravity_prompt: Act as a Senior Product Manager. Help me define 3 core analytics events for a student SaaS project that would impress a hiring manager during an interview.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Analyze my codebase and suggest 3 high-value tracking events based on the current UI.'

steps.title: Initialize Analytics Provider
steps.description: Integrate a lightweight provider like PostHog or Umami to ensure data privacy and ease of use for a beginner developer.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a clean, modular TypeScript wrapper for a PostHog analytics integration that handles event tracking and user identification.
steps.claude_code_command: claude write 'Create a new analytics service file and integrate it into the main app entry point.'

steps.title: Instrument Critical User Flows
steps.description: Place tracking hooks strategically at points of friction or high value, such as form submissions or feature toggles.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review my current form component and suggest where to place tracking events to capture conversion drop-offs.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Instrument the signup form and CTA buttons with tracking events.'

steps.title: Build a Data Dashboard
steps.description: Visualize your data. A dashboard proves you can transform raw events into actionable insights.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a simple dashboard component that fetches recent event data and displays it using a lightweight charting library.
steps.claude_code_command: claude write 'Add a dashboard page that visualizes the tracking data collected in the previous steps.'

steps.title: Documenting the 'Why' for Recruiters
steps.description: Create a README section that explains your analytics architecture. This is what separates a student from a professional.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a professional README section explaining why I chose this analytics stack and how it helps optimize user retention.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Update the project README with an Analytics Implementation section.'

pro_tips: Always use environment variables for your API keys; exposing analytics keys in public repos is a rookie mistake.
Focus on 'Event Properties'—don't just track that a button was clicked, track which specific version or context it was clicked in.
Use a 'staging' environment to verify your analytics events fire correctly before deploying to production.
Don't track everything. Focus on 3-5 high-impact events; tracking too much makes data noise and hurts performance.
When interviewing, mention your analytics setup as a way to prove you think about the product lifecycle, not just the syntax.
content.next_steps: Deploy your project to Vercel or Netlify, share it with 10 friends to generate initial data, and then link your analytics dashboard in your portfolio to showcase your 'Product Mindset'.
content.conversion_block: You are no longer just a student; you are a product engineer who understands data. This is the competitive edge that gets you hired. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
